                               g 
                             is obviously injective (because  
                               
                                  
                                     ≤ 
                                  
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                               
                             is antisymmetric), it follows that  
                               g 
                             is an embedding, and the proof is complete. □

We prove (i)

We conclude this section by deriving some interesting consequences of  Theorem 5.6 . Recall that a family of orderings  
                         Z 
                       is  universal  for an order-theoretic 
                         27 
                      
                      
                         27 
                         A property is  order-theoretic  if it is invariant under order-isomorphisms. 
                       property  
                         P 
                       if each element of  
                         Z 
                       has  
                         P 
                      , and every ordering having  
                         P 
                       embeds into an element of  
                         Z 
                      . Then we have:  
                         Corollary 5.7 
                         
                            
                               Z 
                            
                            -lines are universal semiorders.

Z 
                            
                            -lines are universal semiorders.

Proof

                               Z 
                            -line. □

Apply  Theorem 5.6 : (ii)

The next result is an immediate consequence of  Theorem 5.6  and the universality of  
                         Q 
                       for countable linear orders.  
                         Corollary 5.8 
                         
                            A countable simple preference is a semiorder if and only if it embeds into  
                            
                               Q

                                     Z

                         Corollary 5.9 
                         
                            Rabinovitch, 1978 
                         
                         
                            The dimension of a strict semiorder is at most   3 .

Fig. 3 
                       describes how the three strict linear orders  
                         
                            
                               < 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               < 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               < 
                            
                            
                               3 
                            
                         
                      , defined as in the proof of  Corollary 5.9 , distinguish from each other all the elements of  
                         A 
                         × 
                         Z 
                         × 
                         B 
                       having the same first coordinate. The gray areas, which collect one or two vertical slices together, are arranged in a linear order. Within each gray area, elements are ordered according to either  
                         
                            
                               < 
                            
                            
                               B 
                            
                         
                       (in  
                         
                            
                               < 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               < 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                      ) or its reverse ordering  
                         
                            
                               > 
                            
                            
                               B 
                            
                         
                       (in  
                         
                            
                               < 
                            
                            
                               3 
                            
                         
                      ).

In this paper we have described an arbitrary semiorder as a subordering of a modified lexicographic product of three total preorders, where the chain  
                         
                            ( 
                            Z 
                            , 
                            ≤ 
                            ) 
                         
                       of integers endowed with a shift operator is the middle factor. This modified lexicographic product, called  
                         Z 
                      - product , is characterized by the fact that the lexicographic ordering is respected by the first and the third factor, but the middle factor introduces a threshold of discrimination. We prove that the family of  
                         Z 
                      -products is universal for semiorders, in the sense that  
                         Z 
                      -products are semiorders themselves, and each semiorder embeds into a  
                         Z 
                      -product.

Let us quickly summarize the main steps of the representation of a semiorder by a  
                         Z 
                      -product. Fix a semiordered structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            X 
                            , 
                            ≾ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , and denote by  
                         
                            
                               ≾ 
                            
                            
                               
                                  tc 
                               
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               ≾ 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                       the transitive closure and the trace of  
                         ≾ 
                      , respectively. Let  
                         x 
                         , 
                         y 
                         ∈ 
                         X 
                       be such that  
                         x 
                         ≺ 
                         y 
                      .  
                         
                            
                               Step   1. 
                            Either  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≺ 
                                     
                                     
                                        t 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                or  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                     
                                        t 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                holds. (The case  
                                  y 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≺ 
                                     
                                     
                                        t 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  x 
                                is obviously impossible.) Further, if  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≺ 
                                     
                                     
                                        t 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                               , then  
                                  x 
                                  ≺ 
                                  y 
                               . 
                         
                         
                            
                               Step   2. 
                            In order to analyze what occurs in the case  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                     
                                        t 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                               , a special function  
                                  ζ 
                                  : 
                                  X 
                                  → 
                                  Z 
                                is obtained. This function, called a  linear monotonic integer slicer  ( LMIS ), has the following properties: for each  
                                  u 
                                  , 
                                  v 
                                  ∈ 
                                  X 
                                such that  
                                  u 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∼

                                  v

                                  u

                                     u

                                     ( 
                                     v

                                  ⟹

                                  ≺ 
                                  v

                                  ζ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     u 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  < 
                                  ζ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     v 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ⟹ 
                                  u 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≺ 
                                     
                                     
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                                  v 
                               . The definition of  
                                  ζ 
                                is reminiscent of constructions related to the Scott–Suppes representation of a semiorder. As a consequence, whenever  
                                  x 
                                  ≺ 
                                  y 
                                and  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                     
                                        t 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                holds, then we have either  
                                  ζ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  + 
                                  1 
                                  < 
                                  ζ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     y 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                or  
                                  ζ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  + 
                                  1 
                                  = 
                                  ζ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     y 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               . Moreover,  
                                  ζ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  + 
                                  1 
                                  < 
                                  ζ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     y

                               , combined with  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                     
                                        t 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                               , implies  
                                  x 
                                  ≺ 
                                  y 
                               . 
                         
                         
                            
                               Step   3. 
                            Thus, it remains to establish a procedure that allows us to “distinguish”  
                                  x 
                                from  
                                  y 
                                whenever they are in consecutive slices, that is,  
                                  ζ 
                                  
                                     (

                                  =

                                     ( 
                                     y 
                                     )

                               , called a  sliced trace , which depends on the function  
                                  ζ 
                               . This total preorder, denoted by  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≾ 
                                     
                                     
                                        ζ 
                                     
                                  
                               , has the property that the equivalence  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≺ 
                                     
                                     
                                        ζ 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                  ⟺ 
                                  x 
                                  ≺ 
                                  y 
                                holds for each  
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  X 
                                such that  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           tc 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                and  
                                  ζ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  + 
                                  1 
                                  = 
                                  ζ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     y 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               .

Either  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≺ 
                                     
                                     
                                        t 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                or  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                     
                                        t 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                holds. (The case  
                                  y 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≺ 
                                     
                                     
                                        t 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  x 
                                is obviously impossible.) Further, if  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≺ 
                                     
                                     
                                        t 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                               , then  
                                  x 
                                  ≺ 
                                  y 
                               .

In order to analyze what occurs in the case  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                     
                                        t 
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                               , a special function  
                                  ζ

The main result of this paper characterizes semiorders as those simple preferences that are embeddable in a  
                         Z 
                      -product. In fact, we show that a semiorder embeds in the  
                         Z 
                      -product having the transitive closure of the semiorder as its first factor, and a sliced trace as its third factor. Further, special  
                         Z 
                      -products–called  
                         Z 
                      - lines , and characterized by the fact that the extreme factors are linear orders–turn out to be universal semiorders, too. Finally, as a corollary of the universality of  
                         Z 
                      -lines, we derive that the dimension of a strict semiorder is at most three.

Future research on the topic goes in two main directions. First, we believe that our descriptive approach naturally prompts suitable extensions of several results on semiorders that are scattered throughout the literature. For instance, we conjecture that positive-threshold  GNR  and  GUR  representations of semiorders à la Beja–Gilboa (see Theorems 3.7, 3.8, 4.4, and 4.5 in  Beja & Gilboa, 1992 ) can be obtained as particular cases of more general representations. We also conjecture that Candeal and Induráin’s internal characterization of the Scott–Suppes representability of a semiorder (see Lemma 3.4 and Theorem 3.6 in  Candeal & Induráin, 2010 , as well as Theorem 4.11 and Corollary 4.12 in  Candeal et al., 2012 ) is a special case of forms of utilities with values in a suitable  
                         Z 
                      -line. 
                         28 
                      
                      
                         28 
                         We have just proved that this conjecture holds true: see  Example 5.4 .

In another direction of research, we are currently working on an extension of the descriptive characterization of a semiorder to other quasi-transitive preferences, which satisfy a  weak

                       In the same direction, one could identify universal types of pairs  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  ≾ 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            ,

                      , for instance special types of  NaP-preferences . 
                         31 
                      
                      
                         31 
                         A  NaP-preference  ( necessary and possible preference ) is a pair  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≾ 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≾ 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             of binary relations on the same ground set such that its  necessary  component  
                               
                                  
                                     ≾ 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                             is a preorder, its  possible  component  
                               
                                  
                                     ≾ 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                             is a quasi-transitive completion of the first, and the two relations jointly satisfy suitable forms of transitive coherence and mixed completeness ( Giarlotta, 2014, 2015; Giarlotta & Greco, 2013 ). Under the  Axiom of Choice , a NaP-preference is characterized by the existence of a set of total preorders whose intersection and union give, respectively, the necessary component and the possible component (Theorem 3.4 in  Giarlotta & Greco, 2013 ). Special types of NaP-preferences (having, e.g., a semiorder as possible component) are studied in  Giarlotta (2014) .
